result:
There is a specific kind of silence that only exists just before dawn, right when the world is holding its breath. If you walk to the edge of the ocean at that hour, you don’t hear silence at all—you hear the roar of the tides. It’s a paradox that I’ve always loved: the loudest place on earth can make you feel the most profoundly quiet inside. 

The water doesn't care about your deadlines, your broken heart, or your existential dread. It just pulls and pushes, obeying the ancient gravity of a moon hanging pale and indifferent in the fading sky. There is no negotiation with the sea. It takes what it wants and leaves behind the rest: tangles of kelp, cracked shells, sea glass smoothed by a thousand collisions. 

Sometimes I think we are all just sea glass. We start off sharp, jagged, freshly broken from something larger, tossed into the churning foam of life. We bump against rocks and sand and each other, gradually losing our sharp edges. We think we are being destroyed, but really, we are being polished. We are being made into something smooth enough to let the light pass through. 

Standing on the shoreline with the cold water rushing over my bare feet, the infinite stretch of the horizon reminds me of my own smallness. And instead of feeling insignificant, it feels like relief. The weight of the world is too heavy to carry alone, but the ocean carries it effortlessly. All I have to do is let the tide pull the worry away.
